description: Glial cells are heterogeneous non-neuronal cells essential for neuronal support, myelination, nervous system development, and increasingly recognized roles in sensory, immune, and autonomic functions across the nervous system.
The basic cellular unit of nervous tissue. Each neuron consists of a body, an axon, and dendrites. Their purpose is to receive, conduct, and transmit impulses in the nervous system.
Non-neural, non-glial cell types in the CNS of mesenchymal, endothelial, or epithelial origin
Progenitor cells in the nervous system are self-renewing, multipotent precursors that generate neurons, glia, and related cell types, including both central (CNS) and peripheral (PNS) lineages.

A star-shaped macroglial cell in the CNS; the most abundant glial type. Regulates the extracellular environment, contributes to the blood-brain barrier via end feet, and becomes reactive in response to injury.
A neuron that uses acetylcholine as a vesicular neurotransmitter.

Cerebral cortex cell that processes sensory input and generates motor output; divides into glutamatergic projection neurons and local GABAergic interneurons, forming the circuits underlying cognition, perception and consciousness.
A neuron that releases dopamine as a neurotransmitter.

An endothelial cell comprises the outermost layer or lining of anatomical structures and can be squamous or cuboidal. In mammals, endothelial cell has vimentin filaments and is derived from the mesoderm.

A neuroepithelial glial cell, derived from a radial glial cell originating from the neuroectoderm, lines the ventricles of the brain and the central canal of the spinal cord. This cell is characterized by the presence of cilia on its apical surface, which can be motile or non-motile.

A cell that is usually found in a two-dimensional sheet with a free surface. The cell has a cytoskeleton that allows for tight cell to cell contact and for cell polarity where apical part is directed towards the lumen and the basal part to the basal lamina.
A glutamatergic neuron that enhances postsynaptic activity; originates in the dorsal ventricular zone and populates cortical and subcortical regions.

A connective tissue cell which secretes an extracellular matrix rich in collagen and other macromolecules. Flattened and irregular in outline with branching processes; appear fusiform or spindle-shaped.

A neuron of the vertebrate central nervous system that is small in size. This general class includes small neurons in the granular layer of the cerebellar cortex, cerebral cortex neurons that are not pyramidal cells and small neurons without axons found in the olfactory bulb.
A GABAergic or glycinergic neuron that suppresses postsynaptic firing; derived from ventral ganglionic eminences and integrates into diverse brain circuits to maintain excitation-inhibition balance.

Most generally any neuron which is not motor or sensory. Interneurons may also refer to neurons whose axons remain within a particular brain region as contrasted with projection neurons which have axons projecting to other brain regions.
Mononuclear phagocyte: tissue-resident phagocyte derived from monocytes; clears microbes and debris.
An inhibitory, GABAergic projection neuron in the striatum that integrates glutamatergic signals arising from the cerebral cortex and thalamus.

Structural cell of the three meningeal layers (dura, arachnoid, pia) that secretes collagen and basement-membrane components, forms the epithelial-like arachnoid barrier, and acts as an essential interface between CNS and periphery during development, homeostasis and disease.

Yolk-sac-derived resident macrophages of the central nervous system, comprising ~10% of brain cells. In the healthy state they display a ramified morphology; upon injury or infection they transform into amoeboid phagocytes. Microglia eliminate pathogens and cellular debris, prune excess synapses during development, support tissue repair, and maintain neural homeostasis. Over-activation contributes to neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative diseases.
NG2-expressing glial cells in the CNS; function as oligodendrocyte progenitors and active modulators of neural circuits through neuron-glia interactions, neuromodulation, and neuroprotection.

A cell of the neural crest. Neural crest cells are multipotent. Premigratory neural crest cells are found at the neural plate boarder, some of which will undergo ectomesynchymal transition and delamination to form migratory neural crest cells.

An undifferentiated neural cell that originates from the neuroectoderm and has the capacity both to perpetually self-renew without differentiating and to generate multiple central nervous system neuronal and glial cell types.
A cell that will develop into a neuron often after a migration phase.
A cell of a platelike structure, especially a thickened plate of ectoderm in the early embryo, from which a sense organ develops.
A class of large neuroglial (macroglial) cells in the central nervous system. Form the insulating myelin sheath of axons in the central nervous system.

A proliferative and migratory glial progenitor cell that derives from a neural stem cell and resides within the central nervous system. It possesses the capacity to differentiate into a committed oligodendrocyte progenitor (COP) through a well-defined series of maturation steps, ultimately giving rise to a myelinating oligodendrocyte (MOL).

An elongated, contractile cell found wrapped about precapillary arterioles outside the basement membrane. Pericytes are present in capillaries where proper adventitia and muscle layer are missing (thus distingushing this cell type from adventitial cells). They are relatively undifferentiated and may become fibroblasts, macrophages, or smooth muscle cells.

Pyramidal neurons have a pyramid-shaped soma with a single axon, a large apical dendrite and multiple basal dendrites. The apex and an apical dendrite typically point toward the pial surface and other dendrites and an axon emerging from the base. The axons may have local collaterals but also project outside their region. Pyramidal neurons are found in the cerebral cortex, the hippocampus, and the amygdala.
A cell present in the developing CNS. Functions as both a precursor cell and as a scaffold to support neuronal migration.
Any neuron having a sensory function; an afferent neuron conveying sensory impulses.
A neuron that releases serotonin as a neurotransmitter.
A non-striated, elongated, spindle-shaped cell found lining the digestive tract, uterus, and blood vessels. They develop from specialized myoblasts (smooth muscle myoblast).

Rare, highly proliferative cell committed to endothelial lineage; capable of colony formation and vascular integration; mediates vessel repair chiefly via paracrine signals rather than long-term engraftment.
Any neuron that has its soma located in some amygdala and is capable of some glutamate secretion, neurotransmission.
Star-shaped glial cell with end feet contributing to the blood-brain barrier; regulates extracellular homeostasis and becomes reactive after injury.
Cerebellar radial astrocyte that spans Purkinje to pia, buffers glutamate and sculpts synapses.
A tissue-resident macrophage found in the central nervous system.

Cerebellar inhibitory interneurons are GABAergic and/or glycinergic neurons derived from Ptf1a? progenitors in the embryonic cerebellar ventricular neuroepithelium. Though a minority of cerebellar neurons, they critically regulate circuit activity¡ªtypically inhibiting target cells, though GABA can be excitatory early in development.
GABAergic neuron born in the caudal ganglionic eminence;accounts for ¡Ý50% of human cortical inhibitory neurons; mediates disinhibition and cognitive processing.

A GABAergic interneuron that selectively innervates the axon initial segment of pyramidal cells. Their local axonal clusters are formed by high-frequency branching at shallow angles, often ramifying around, above or below their somata with a high bouton density. The characteristic terminal portions of the axon form short vertical rows of boutons, resembling the candlesticks and candles of a chandelier. Chandelier cells can be multipolar or bitufted.

A specialized ependymal cell that is part of the choroid plexus epithelium, responsible for producing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) by selectively filtering and modifying blood plasma components before secreting it into the brain and spinal cord. This cell is characterized by a brush border on its apical surface, which enhances the secretion of CSF.
An oligodendrocyte precursor cell that is committed to differentiate.
L5 thick-tufted pyramidal neuron projecting to thalamus, superior colliculus and brainstem; intrinsically bursting, direction-selective, high-temporal-frequency preference.
L5 thin-tufted pyramidal neuron with bilateral cortical and striatal axons; regular-spiking, intermediate spatial-frequency tuning, mediates inter-hemispheric feedforward.
L5 oval-soma pyramidal neuron restricted to adjacent ipsilateral cortex, no striatal/subcortical collaterals; high input resistance, high spatial-frequency preference, local output channel.

A medium spiny neuron that exhibits transcriptional divergence from direct and indirect spiny projection neurons, for example, enrichment in Casz1, Otof, Cacng5 and Pcdh8 noted in mice. Whilst in general medium spiny neurons have been found to be differentially distributed across the basal ganglia, the eccentric medium spiny neuron cell type has been found to be more evenly distributed throughout cerebral nuclei.

An endothelial cell comprises the outermost layer or lining of anatomical structures and can be squamous or cuboidal. In mammals, endothelial cell has vimentin filaments and is derived from the mesoderm.

A neuroepithelial glial cell, derived from a radial glial cell originating from the neuroectoderm, lines the ventricles of the brain and the central canal of the spinal cord. This cell is characterized by the presence of cilia on its apical surface, which can be motile or non-motile.

A connective tissue cell which secretes an extracellular matrix rich in collagen and other macromolecules. Flattened and irregular in outline with branching processes; appear fusiform or spindle-shaped.

A glutamatergic principal neuron located in the polymorphic hilus of the dentate gyrus that receives excitatory input from granule cells and provides extensive feedback projections to both granule cells and local GABAergic interneurons, thereby regulating dentate excitability and anxiety-related information routing.

Hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons are glutamatergic principal neurons located in the CA1 subfield of the hippocampus that play a central role in spatial navigation and mnemonic processing. In humans, they are part of an evolutionarily reorganized hippocampal circuit with altered cytoarchitecture and expanded size compared to other primates. These neurons exhibit intrinsic electrophysiological properties¡ªsuch as membrane resonance¡ªthat are thought to support the generation and entrainment of hippocampal theta oscillations (1¨C4 Hz in humans, slower than the 4¨C10 Hz in rodents).

Hippocampal CA2 pyramidal neurons are a distinct subpopulation of glutamatergic principal neurons located in the CA2 subfield of the hippocampus. They are molecularly unique, characterized by a robust and selective gene expression profile, and are the only glutamatergic neurons among hippocampal CA fields that are ensheathed by perineuronal nets (PNNs).

An excitatory projection neuron located in the stratum pyramidale of the hippocampal CA3 region, characterized by dense recurrent collateral axons that form an autoassociative network. It exhibits a proximodistal gradient in intrinsic excitability and synaptic connectivity, with CA3b displaying the strongest net recurrent excitation and memory reactivation. CA3 neurons receive mossy-fiber input from the dentate gyrus and direct perforant-path input from the entorhinal cortex, and are essential for associative memory storage, pattern completion, and memory recall.

A granule cell that has soma location in the dentate gyrus cell layer of the hippocampal formation and has an elliptical cell body and characteristic cone-shaped tree of spiny apical dendrites. The branches extend throughout the molecular layer and the distal tips of the dendritic tree end just at the hippocampal fissure or at the ventricular surface. The dentate gyrus granule cell is the principal cell type of the dentate gyrus.
An inhibitory, GABAergic projection neuron in the striatum that integrates glutamatergic signals arising from the cerebral cortex and thalamus.

MGE-derived interneurons are a transient embryonic population born in the medial ganglionic eminence (MGE) that express the transcription factors Lhx6 and Nkx2.1, migrate to cortex and striatum between E12.5¨CP10, and mature into fast-spiking GABAergic or tonically active cholinergic interneurons essential for balancing excitation/inhibition and shaping functional circuits.

Microglia are the primary resident immune cells of the central nervous system (CNS), constituting approximately 10% of all CNS cells. Originating from yolk sac progenitors, they differ from other glial cells (such as astrocytes and oligodendrocytes), which arise from neuroectodermal lineages. Microglia serve as the first line of immune defense in the CNS, expressing a wide array of pattern recognition receptors that enable them to detect and eliminate pathogens, as well as perform phagocytosis of cellular debris or microbes. In the resting state, they exhibit a ramified morphology; upon activation by injury or inflammation, they transform into an amoeboid shape. Beyond their immune functions, microglia also participate in synaptic pruning during development, removal of superfluous neurons, tissue repair, and maintenance of neural homeostasis. Although generally protective, their overactivation can contribute to neuroinflammatory conditions and is strongly implicated in neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer¡¯s disease, Parkinson¡¯s disease, and multiple sclerosis.
A GABAergic inhibitory neuron that is derived from the midbrain.

A cell of the neural crest. Neural crest cells are multipotent. Premigratory neural crest cells are found at the neural plate boarder, some of which will undergo ectomesynchymal transition and delamination to form migratory neural crest cells.
A cell that will develop into a neuron often after a migration phase.
A cell of a platelike structure, especially a thickened plate of ectoderm in the early embryo, from which a sense organ develops.
A class of large neuroglial (macroglial) cells in the central nervous system. Form the insulating myelin sheath of axons in the central nervous system.

A proliferative and migratory glial progenitor cell that derives from a neural stem cell and resides within the central nervous system. It possesses the capacity to differentiate into a committed oligodendrocyte progenitor (COP) through a well-defined series of maturation steps, ultimately giving rise to a myelinating oligodendrocyte (MOL). In mice and humans, it is characterized by the expression of specific molecular markers, including Pdgfra, Cspg4 (also known as NG2) and Olig2.

An elongated, contractile cell found wrapped about precapillary arterioles outside the basement membrane. Pericytes are present in capillaries where proper adventitia and muscle layer are missing (thus distingushing this cell type from adventitial cells). They are relatively undifferentiated and may become fibroblasts, macrophages, or smooth muscle cells.
A cell present in the developing CNS. Functions as both a precursor cell and as a scaffold to support neuronal migration.

An excitatory neuron that has its soma located in the thalamic complex. This neuron type can be involved in a series of circuits related to sensory integration, motor integration, pain processing, social behaviour and reward response.

Vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) are the predominant cellular component of the medial layer of blood vessel walls, responsible for maintaining vascular tone, elasticity, and structural integrity. Under physiological conditions, VSMCs exhibit a contractile phenotype, characterized by the expression of contractile proteins such as ¦Á-smooth muscle actin (ACTA2), transgelin (TAGLN), calponin, and myosin heavy chain 11 (MYH11), and they rarely proliferate or migrate.
In pathological states such as atherosclerosis, VSMCs undergo phenotypic switching, transitioning from a contractile to a synthetic, inflammatory, or foam cell phenotype. This plasticity enables VSMCs to proliferate, migrate into the intima, synthesize extracellular matrix, and take up lipids, contributing to plaque formation, fibrous cap development, and plaque stability or rupture. Their role is central in both the initiation and progression of atherosclerosis.

A midbrain dopaminergic neuron that has its soma located in a substantia nigra. This dopaminergic neuron type is highly metabolically active and it is involved in the regulation of movement, cognition, motivation and reward. Neurodegeneration of this dopaminergic neuronal type causes loss in fine motor control in Parkinson's Disease.

Basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (BFCNs) are magnocellular, acetylcholine-producing neurons forming a discontinuous band from septum to substantia innominata and nucleus basalis, projecting densely to cortex, hippocampus and amygdala to regulate attention, learning and memory; they are selectively vulnerable in AD, LBD and TDP-43 proteinopathies, and their degeneration underlies the ¡°cholinergic hypothesis¡± of dementia.
Dorsal raphe serotonergic neurons (DRN 5-HT neurons) are serotonin-producing cells located in the dorsal raphe nucleus that project widely to forebrain regions.
A sensory neuron of the dorsal root ganglia that senses body position and sends information about how much the muscle is stretched to the spinal cord.

Polydendrocytes (NG2 glia) are a distinct resident glial population that uniformly occupy gray and white matter of the developing and adult CNS, express NG2 proteoglycan and PDGF¦Á receptor, and function primarily as oligodendrocyte precursor cells capable of lifelong self-renewal and activity-dependent myelin remodeling while also displaying lineage plasticity to generate astrocytes during development and responding to injury.

An undifferentiated neural cell that originates from the neuroectoderm and has the capacity both to perpetually self-renew without differentiating and to generate multiple central nervous system neuronal and glial cell types.

Dura cells constitute the outermost meningeal fibroblast population lining the inner calvarial surface and are essential for intracranial mechanical homeostasis and immune surveillance. They segregate into two molecularly and functionally distinct subtypes:
Outer-dural cells express MSX2, HHIP and COL2A1, remain continuous with the periosteum, and provide structural support as well as osteomeningeal linkage.
Inner-dural cells express SLC47A1, CDH1 and a full complement of tight-junction proteins (OCLN, CLDNs, TJs, etc.), display barrier-cell ultrastructure, and collaborate with the arachnoid barrier to establish the meninges¨Cblood interface.

The intermediate layer of epithelial-like cells located between the dura mater and pia mater highly expresses SLC22A6/SLC22A2 and CDH1, and forms the arachnoid barrier through dense tight junctions (CLDNs, OCLN, JAMs, etc.), physically separating the cerebrospinal fluid from the venous blood. This barrier regulates the bidirectional transport of solutes and water, maintaining the homeostasis of the central extracellular fluid, while simultaneously secreting signaling molecules such as PTGDS to participate in intracranial immune regulation.

The innermost layer of flattened fibroblast-like cells, closely apposed to the surface of the brain-spinal cord parenchyma, originates from the embryonic primary meninx and specifically expresses LAMC3. These cells fuse with the basement membrane of cerebral blood vessels to form the outer wall of the Virchow-Robin space, providing mechanical and molecular support to the vessels. Additionally, they regulate neural development, angiogenesis, and immune cell trafficking into the parenchyma through the secretion of laminins and growth factors.

Vascular endothelial progenitor cells are a class of precursor cells that possess the capacity for self-renewal and the potential to differentiate into functional blood vessels with an intimal layer, and have been validated by serial transplantation experiments to stably engraft and reconstitute the host vascular system.
